---
title: "JSON Files Analysis"
author: "Your Name"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

## Loading Required Libraries

```{r libraries}
library(jsonlite)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(knitr)
library(DT)
library(here)
```

## Reading JSON Files

```{r read-json-files}
# Set the path to your folder containing JSON files
# Change this path to match your folder location
setwd(here::here())

folder_path <- "data/results"

# Get list of all JSON files in the folder
json_files <- list.files(folder_path, pattern = "*.json", full.names = TRUE)

cat("Found", length(json_files), "JSON files\n")

# Read all JSON files into a list
json_data <- lapply(json_files, function(file) {
  tryCatch({
    fromJSON(file)
  }, error = function(e) {
    cat("Error reading file:", file, "\n")
    return(NULL)
  })
})

# Remove any NULL entries (failed reads)
json_data <- json_data[!sapply(json_data, is.null)]

cat("Successfully read", length(json_data), "files\n")
```

## Converting to Data Frame

```{r create-dataframe}
# Convert list to data frame
df_raw <- do.call(rbind, lapply(json_data, data.frame, stringsAsFactors = FALSE))

# Check the structure
str(df_raw)
head(df_raw)
```

## Reshaping Data

```{r reshape-data}
# Simple approach - just get it done
result_df <- df_raw %>%
  select(story, prompt, answer) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = prompt, values_from = answer, names_prefix = "prompt_")

# Convert to data frame and set row names
result_df <- as.data.frame(result_df)
rownames(result_df) <- result_df$story
result_df$story <- NULL

# Show the result
print(result_df)
```

## Bible Stories Clustering Analysis

```{r clustering-setup}
# Load required libraries
library(cluster)
library(factoextra)
library(pheatmap)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Assuming your data frame is called 'result_df' and contains TRUE/FALSE values
# Convert to numeric for clustering (TRUE = 1, FALSE = 0)

result_df <- result_df[complete.cases(result_df), ]
data_numeric <- as.data.frame(lapply(result_df, as.numeric))
rownames(data_numeric) <- rownames(result_df)
```

## Method 1: K-means Clustering (Most Common)

```{r kmeans-clustering}
# Determine optimal number of clusters using elbow method
set.seed(123)
wss <- sapply(1:10, function(k){
  kmeans(data_numeric, centers = k, nstart = 25)$tot.withinss
})

# Plot elbow curve
plot(1:10, wss, type = "b", pch = 19, 
     xlab = "Number of Clusters", ylab = "Within-cluster Sum of Squares",
     main = "Elbow Method for Optimal k")

# Try k-means with different numbers of clusters
k <- 4  # Adjust based on elbow plot
kmeans_result <- kmeans(data_numeric, centers = k, nstart = 25)

# Add cluster assignments to your data
result_df$cluster <- kmeans_result$cluster

# Show cluster assignments
print("Cluster assignments:")
table(kmeans_result$cluster)

# Show stories in each cluster
for(i in 1:k) {
  cat("\nCluster", i, "stories:\n")
  cat(paste(rownames(result_df)[result_df$cluster == i], collapse = ", "), "\n")
}
```

## Method 2: Hierarchical Clustering (Better for interpretation)

```{r hierarchical-clustering}
# Calculate distance matrix using method suitable for binary data
# Jaccard distance is good for binary data
dist_matrix <- dist(data_numeric, method = "binary")  # Uses Jaccard distance

# Alternative distance methods for binary data:
# dist_matrix <- dist(data_numeric, method = "manhattan")  # Manhattan distance
# dist_matrix <- cluster::daisy(data_numeric, metric = "jaccard")  # Explicit Jaccard

# Perform hierarchical clustering
hclust_result <- hclust(dist_matrix, method = "ward.D2")

# Plot dendrogram
plot(hclust_result, main = "Hierarchical Clustering of Bible Stories", 
     xlab = "", sub = "", cex = 0.8)

# Cut tree to get clusters
h_clusters <- cutree(hclust_result, k = 4)  # Adjust number of clusters as needed

# Add to dataframe
result_df$h_cluster <- h_clusters

# Show hierarchical cluster assignments
for(i in 1:max(h_clusters)) {
  cat("\nHierarchical Cluster", i, "stories:\n")
  cat(paste(rownames(result_df)[result_df$h_cluster == i], collapse = ", "), "\n")
}
```

## Method 3: PAM Clustering (Robust alternative)

```{r pam-clustering}
# PAM (Partitioning Around Medoids) is more robust to outliers
# Good for binary data
pam_result <- pam(data_numeric, k = 4, metric = "manhattan")

result_df$pam_cluster <- pam_result$clustering

# Show PAM cluster assignments
for(i in 1:4) {
  cat("\nPAM Cluster", i, "stories:\n")
  cat(paste(rownames(result_df)[result_df$pam_cluster == i], collapse = ", "), "\n")
}

# Show medoids (representative stories for each cluster)
cat("\nCluster medoids (representative stories):\n")
print(rownames(data_numeric)[pam_result$medoids])
```

## Visualization

```{r clustering-visualization}
# 1. Heatmap showing patterns
pheatmap(as.matrix(data_numeric), 
         clustering_distance_rows = "binary",
         clustering_method = "ward.D2",
         show_colnames = FALSE,
         main = "Bible Stories Clustering Heatmap",
         fontsize_row = 8)

# 2. PCA visualization (even though data is binary, can still be informative)
pca_result <- prcomp(data_numeric, scale = TRUE)
pca_data <- data.frame(
  PC1 = pca_result$x[,1],
  PC2 = pca_result$x[,2],
  Story = rownames(data_numeric),
  Cluster = factor(result_df$h_cluster)
)

ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Cluster, label = Story)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(vjust = -0.5, hjust = 0.5, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Bible Stories Clusters (PCA Visualization)",
       x = paste0("PC1 (", round(summary(pca_result)$importance[2,1] * 100, 1), "% variance)"),
       y = paste0("PC2 (", round(summary(pca_result)$importance[2,2] * 100, 1), "% variance)")) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Cluster Analysis and Interpretation

```{r cluster-analysis}
# Analyze what makes each cluster unique
# Find the most characteristic features for each cluster
analyze_cluster_features <- function(cluster_num) {
  cluster_stories <- result_df$h_cluster == cluster_num
  cluster_data <- data_numeric[cluster_stories, ]
  
  # Calculate proportion of TRUE for each feature in this cluster
  cluster_props <- colMeans(cluster_data)
  
  # Calculate proportion of TRUE for each feature overall
  overall_props <- colMeans(data_numeric)
  
  # Find features that are notably higher in this cluster
  enriched_features <- cluster_props - overall_props
  
  # Get top features that characterize this cluster
  top_features <- names(sort(enriched_features, decreasing = TRUE))[1:10]
  
  cat("\nCluster", cluster_num, "- Most characteristic features:\n")
  for(feature in top_features) {
    cat(sprintf("%s: %.2f (vs %.2f overall, +%.2f)\n", 
                feature, 
                cluster_props[feature], 
                overall_props[feature], 
                enriched_features[feature]))
  }
}

# Analyze each cluster
for(i in 1:max(result_df$h_cluster)) {
  analyze_cluster_features(i)
}
```

## Summary and Recommendations

```{r clustering-summary}
# Compare different clustering methods
comparison <- data.frame(
  Story = rownames(result_df),
  KMeans = result_df$cluster,
  Hierarchical = result_df$h_cluster,
  PAM = result_df$pam_cluster
)

print("Clustering method comparison:")
print(comparison)

# Calculate silhouette scores to evaluate clustering quality
sil_kmeans <- silhouette(kmeans_result$cluster, dist_matrix)
sil_hierarchical <- silhouette(result_df$h_cluster, dist_matrix)
sil_pam <- silhouette(pam_result$clustering, dist_matrix)

cat("\nClustering quality (average silhouette width):\n")
cat("K-means:", mean(sil_kmeans[,3]), "\n")
cat("Hierarchical:", mean(sil_hierarchical[,3]), "\n")
cat("PAM:", mean(sil_pam[,3]), "\n")

# The method with the highest silhouette score is generally better
```
